summary: Visions & Foolish Heart shine; jam‑packed set with stellar jams
blurb: The show delivers a monster first set loaded with new material and tight playing. Highlights include a soaring "Visions Of Johanna," a fiery "Foolish Heart" and a marathon Truckin' → I Just Want To Make Love To You → That Would Be Something jam that rolls into an inspired drum/space segment. The second half stays high‑energy with Vince’s electric piano adding warmth throughout.
review: February 21, 1995 at the Delta Center captured one of the Dead’s most exuberant Utah outings. Jerry was in top form – his soaring guitar tone and emotive vocals on "Visions Of Johanna" and "Foolish Heart" were repeatedly praised, while his lead on the I‑Just‑Want‑To‑Make‑Love‑To‑You segue added a playful edge. Phil’s bass stayed deep and grooving despite occasional mix burial, and his harmonies on "Truckin'" helped lock the band together. Bob’s vocal delivery was strong, especially on the titular "Salt Lake City," and he sounded delightfully spacey during the jam sections. Vince Welnick’s electric piano shone on classics like "Friend Of The Devil" and "So Many Roads," providing a full, comforting backdrop that many reviewers called a highlight. Mickey and Bill turned the drum/space segment into a 15‑minute showcase of heavy cymbals, tight interplay, and pure rhythmic joy – a point of consensus across all recordings. While one audience source suffered from poor fidelity, the soundboard captures (particularly the Miller SBD) are crisp, low‑noise, and bring out every nuance of this high‑energy performance, making it a must‑listen for fans of the mid‑90s era.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of the five recordings are described with positive language (e.g., "legendary," "stellar," "joyous"). Reviewers repeatedly highlight the same songs – Visions Of Johanna, Foolish Heart, Truckin', Friend Of The Devil – and praise the drum/space segment, Vince's keyboards, and Jerry’s guitar. Only one source is mixed, noting a mellow vibe but still calling the show well‑played. The consensus of strong positive sentiment across multiple recordings justifies a +1.6 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crisp, low‑noise soundboard capture with excellent clarity; highlights the band’s tight playing and includes the celebrated Truckin' → I Just Want To Make Love To You → That Would Be Something jam.

key_highlights: Visions Of Johanna delivers an emotive, soaring performance.
Foolish Heart showcases Jerry’s fiery lead and vocal passion.
Truckin' segues into a marathon jam with I Just Want To Make Love To You and That Would Be Something, capped by a 15‑minute drum/space segment.
Vince Welnick’s electric piano adds warmth on Friend Of The Devil and So Many Roads.
song_highlights: I Just Want To Make Love To You

band_performance.Jerry: Soaring guitar tone and emotive vocals on Visions Of Johanna and Foolish Heart; strong lead on the I Just Want To Make Love To You segue, generally present and energetic throughout.
band_performance.Phil: Solid groove and deep bass foundation; harmonies on Truckin' praised despite occasional mix burial.
band_performance.Bob: Strong vocal delivery especially on Salt Lake City; rhythm work tight and his spacey vibe adds character to the jam sections.
band_performance.Brent: Vince's electric piano shines, delivering full, harmonious sound on Friend Of The Devil, So Many Roads, and throughout the set, with standout solos that lift the performance.
band_performance.Mickey: Incredible drum segment highlighted by heavy cymbal work; leads a 15‑minute drums/space showcase praised as a high point of the night.
band_performance.Billy: Tight drumming that locks in with Mickey; contributes powerfully to the extended jam and maintains steady drive throughout.

must_listen_sequences: I Just Want To Make Love To You
